title: SEGMENT 9 — The instrument family: iSeq, MiSeq, NextSeq, NovaSeq, NovaSeq X

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — The instrument family: iSeq, MiSeq, NextSeq, NovaSeq, NovaSeq X
Illumina sells a family of instruments at very different scales, and which one you pick depends entirely on what you want to do.

They're all built on the same chemistry — the same four-step cycle, the same reversible terminators, the same flow cell paradigm — but they differ enormously in throughput, run time, cost per base, and physical footprint.
Let me walk you through the family, smallest to largest.
The iSeq.
This is the smallest Illumina instrument, introduced around 2018.
Roughly the size of a small countertop appliance — think a heavy toaster oven.

It uses a single-use cartridge, costs maybe twenty thousand dollars to purchase, and produces about 1.2 gigabases per run — enough for a small bacterial genome, a small targeted panel, or a teaching exercise.
Run time is a day or two.
The iSeq is designed for very small labs, individual researchers, or specific point-of-use applications where you just need a little bit of sequence on demand.
It's the only Illumina instrument that uses a CMOS-chip-based imaging system rather than a separate camera, which is part of how it stays small and cheap.
The MiSeq.
The MiSeq is the workhorse for small to medium labs.
It's been around since 2011 and has gone through several generations.
About the size of a small desktop computer.
Costs around a hundred thousand dollars.
Produces about 15 gigabases per run — enough for a few small genomes, a 16S microbiome study, a small RNA-seq experiment, or a clinical panel of dozens of patients.
Run time is about a day.
Reads up to 2 by 300 bp — actually the longest reads in the Illumina family, because MiSeq users tend to need reads long enough to span amplicons and small targets.
The MiSeq is the instrument you'll see in clinical labs doing inherited disease panels, in microbiology labs doing amplicon sequencing, in academic labs doing focused projects.
The NextSeq.
Sits in the middle.
The current NextSeq 1000 and 2000 produce a few hundred gigabases per run — enough for several dozen exomes, a hundred-sample RNA-seq study, or a few low-coverage genomes.
Run time around a day.
Cost in the few hundred thousand dollar range to buy, and consumables a fraction of NovaSeq cost per sample.
The NextSeq is the right instrument for a core facility serving a small institution, or for a clinical lab doing exomes or panels at modest volume.
The NovaSeq 6000.
Until recently, this was Illumina's flagship for big genomics.
About the size of a large copier.
Costs close to a million dollars.

Produces about 6 terabases per run — enough for around 50 human genomes at 30x coverage, or thousands of exomes, or hundreds of bulk RNA-seq libraries, or tens of thousands of single-cell RNA-seq libraries.
Run time about a day and a half.
The NovaSeq 6000 was the workhorse of population-scale sequencing for the late 2010s and early 2020s.
If you've seen a study with thousands of samples, it was probably run on NovaSeqs.
The NovaSeq X.
Launched in September 2022.
The current flagship.
Bigger, faster, denser.
Produces about 16 terabases per run — almost three times what the NovaSeq 6000 does.
Reduces cost per genome to roughly two hundred dollars.
Uses new chemistry called XLEAP-SBS that's faster per cycle, and new flow cells with patterned wells at higher density.
A single NovaSeq X can sequence around twenty thousand human genomes per year.
Targeted at large genome centers, national sequencing programs, big pharmaceutical companies, large clinical genomics operations.
Refrigerator-sized, costly to purchase — over a million dollars — but per-base cost has never been lower.
Here's the analogy that makes the family make sense.
The Illumina instrument family is like an espresso machine product line.
The iSeq is the home pod machine — one cup at a time, slow, but cheap and small enough to sit on a kitchen counter.
The MiSeq is the entry-level commercial machine for a small cafe — handles dozens of cups a day.
The NextSeq is the mid-tier commercial machine for a busy restaurant.
The NovaSeq is the industrial machine for a high-volume hotel banquet operation.
The NovaSeq X is the machine that supplies an entire stadium concession.
Same fundamental espresso chemistry.
Same beans, same hot water, same pressure.
But the throughput, the price, the physical footprint scale across roughly four orders of magnitude depending on which one you pick.
The right instrument depends on your use case.
A boutique clinical lab doing rare disease panels on a few hundred patients per year: MiSeq, maybe NextSeq.
A core facility supporting an academic institution: NextSeq for routine work, with access to a NovaSeq elsewhere for big projects.
A national sequencing center sequencing a population study: NovaSeq X.
A field epidemiologist doing pathogen surveillance during an outbreak: iSeq or MiSeq, depending on volume.

The platform decision is mostly throughput economics: you want the smallest instrument that meets your monthly volume, because consumable costs scale with run size and a NovaSeq X run for ten samples is enormously more expensive per sample than a MiSeq run for ten samples.
There's also an emerging consideration around turnaround time.
For some clinical applications — say, rapidly sequencing a baby in the NICU with an undiagnosed disease — you want the fastest possible run.
Smaller instruments can complete a run in a few hours; the big NovaSeq runs take a day or more.
So even when your total volume might justify a big machine, the time-to-result consideration can push toward a smaller, faster instrument.
Illumina has a specific instrument called the NextSeq 550Dx and a rapid mode on certain platforms targeted at this clinical use case.
The instrument family is also the lens through which Illumina makes most of its money.
Most clinical labs and big sequencing facilities buy the instruments outright — that's a one-time big-ticket purchase.
But the real revenue model is the consumables: the flow cells, the reagents, the cluster generation kits, the sequencing kits.
Every run consumes a kit, and the kits are priced to keep margins high.
Illumina's business model is, structurally, the razor-and-blades model — sell the razor at a reasonable price, make the money on the blades, forever.

The patents on the consumable chemistry are what protect that revenue stream, and we'll come back to the patent question in Segment 18, because some of those patents have expired or are about to expire, and that's reshaping the competitive landscape.
